Should I go through the traditional way that Jeff does it with the internal launch or should I start with maybe a seed launch or whatever? Any time you're trying to prove a concept and that's what you're trying to do here. Everybody on this call probably has an offer that they want to get out there. Any time you want to test out a new offer, you always would like to go with a seed launch. It's what I call the proof of concept launch. It really tells us if your concept is going to work or not. And it tells you whether it's going to work or not quickly. So the idea is to really build up a list and I like to use the magic number of about 300 people, and then put an offer in front of them. And with a seed launch, we don't have to get into these four video types of situations. We only have to do really two videos if we're going to do videos at all. The first video encompasses all the pre-launch content and then the second video is your sales video. And you're making an offer in the sales video and you're sending them to a buy button. And the goal here is to see if somebody is going to take out their credit card and actually purchase this from you. And if you build up the list of 300 people and you get 20, 30, 40 people to buy, you hit the jackpot. You know, you prove the concept that people want to buy your offer of what you have. And this is great news. The other great news is if you put that offer out there and you get no sales, this is great news.

## [24:13 - 27:14]

And the reason it's great news is because you know your offer didn't work and you could pivot to another direction a lot sooner than later. Many times people say to me, I've been working on my website for a year now or six months and I put my whole program together and I'm about to release it and then they go and release it and nobody buys. And they're like, oh my God, I just spent all this time building all this up and nobody purchased. And what I'm saying is a seed launch avoids all of that because if nobody buys, you know you have an offer that nobody's interested in, so why are you going to put all that extra effort into building a site and everything else that goes around it for an offer that's not going to convert? ## [36:21 - 39:36]

But is there a benchmark for success, per se, when you launch a campaign? For example, we've been historically doing a lot of webinars that we will sometimes sell, sometimes we offer those for free. But I can see a relatively low percentage of about 3% to 5% that actually will take the webinar from the list that I marketed that to. So I guess once you guys set up the PLF system, do you have a benchmark that you say that was a successful campaign if it gets a range between 5% or 10% or 10% to 20%? Yeah, unfortunately, the answer to that question is extremely difficult because it's so different from market to market. And it's going to really be about what you feel the success is for your market. When I hear 3% to 5%, I actually think that's good. But it might not be good for your market for all I know. But in a lot of markets, 3% to 5% is a good number. So that's why I say it's hard for me to tell what that is. You're going to have some goals that you and your company are going to put together on what would be a success. You're going to put a launch out there. You're going to get those numbers back. The good news with launches when they work well, you're going to do more in that open cart week. Let's say you're doing it for five or seven days. You should be doing more that week than what you've done for most of the year in any other type of promotion. That's a good way to measure the success. You would know that, oh my God, we bought in all these sales in this one week of open cart compared to what we've been doing for the rest of this whole year leading up to this. If we add all that up and compare it to what we did this week, let's look at what that ratio is. That's a good way to measure it for sure. And then you can improve upon from launch to launch. And number two, I'm getting a little advanced here, so I apologize for people on the call, but I get a little excited about this stuff. Number two, you can create look-alike audiences out of that list, so it'll target other people that look like those buyers on Facebook and present those winning products that you have to them. Oh, another way to build custom audiences is make sure that all the pages on your website have Facebook pixels on them, so even when they visit a page on your site, whether it's a product or the main page of the website, make sure the pixel is firing and Facebook is tracking people that are visiting because then you could target visitors too. And you could target visitors according to product or according to your main page, and then you can again set up retargeting for those audiences and or create look-alike audiences to target people that look like those people that are coming to the page. Well, I do a few things. My main work is paper relief sculptures, but I want to start by making, by selling prints from the sculptures, because that would be a lot easier at this point. Yeah, so the best thing with artists, and this is why I asked who else on the call is an artist, and there's quite a few actually, the strategy, the launch strategy, which is what your question was in the box here for artists, is really taking the potential customers behind the scene of your process, really having them see what it is that you're doing, and almost creating that piece of art with them, and them seeing how it's done through the story arc of these pre-launched pieces of content. And I'll give an example, not everybody on the call here will know the rock band Foo Fighters, but if you do, you'll understand the concept. So the Foo Fighters is a very popular rock band, and with the way music has been going these days, it's been very difficult for musicians and bands to really grab market share because of the new digital age now, and people are not buying CDs or vinyl and that stuff anymore, and everybody's downloading stuff for free and streaming, so they have to get creative. Michael Ward says he loves the Foo Fighters. Well, Michael Ward will know that the Foo Fighters did a couple of different things over the past few years, where they put out a documentary on, I think, Netflix called Back and Forth, and more recently on HBO, they did a six-part series called The Sonic Highways, and both of these, the documentary and the series on HBO, really went into the creation process of them creating their album. The Back and Forth was about their whole career, but it was about how they created their latest album at the time, where they recorded it out of their garage, and it literally, the video took you into their garage, showed you how they set up their tape machines, how they set up each instrument, how they were creating each song, how they put together the song, how they produced the song. It was the whole creation process. Sonic Highways was more about how they created a song in each place that they went to visit.

## [1:28:18 - 1:31:23]

They wanted to get the music history of, let's say, New Orleans or New York, and they would interview a lot of the top musicians from those areas, and as they interviewed them, in those interviews, the lead singer was coming up with lyrics for new songs, and he was showing you how he created a new song from interviewing the musicians. He was taking you behind the scenes of the process, and these were all launches. They might be more commercialized, but when you do a six-part series on HBO, and then you release an album called Sonic Highways, that was a launch sequence. That's what it is. So it's the same thing I'm telling for you and other artists. You want to take people behind the scenes to your craft. Okay. Makes sense? Yeah, that makes perfect sense. So you could use pre-launch content to build your list and to sell the actual product at the end? Yeah, I mean, you could. How can an artist create an evergreen product? Well, it's the same as any other product. I mean, once you have that product out there and you've proven your offer, you can set it to go on evergreen where any new person that's coming into your list is delivered the sequence that ultimately is going to sell them your offer when done. And that's something I don't recommend anybody doing until they have a proven offer. So that doesn't matter whether you're an artist or any product or service that anybody on this call is delivering. To turn something into evergreen is something that anybody could do, you want to make sure that you have a proven offer that's working. Because your evergreen sequence is not going to convert as high as your launch sequence is. So there's going to be a drop-off. And you certainly don't want to be creating an evergreen from scratch if we don't know that it's worked in the first place. So that's the first thing you want to make sure, that it's going to work, and then you could set it to evergreen.
